---
pagetitle: "Jumbo Squid"
page-layout: full
title-block-style: none
toc: false
meta-author: "Juan-Carlos Quiroz"
meta-date: "2026-07-12"
meta-affiliation: "FACILEVISUAL"
---

::: {.hero}
::: {.hero-content}
<div class="hero-title">Jumbo Squid</div>

Why three stock assessments of the same squid disagree — and how probabilistic
risk analysis can reconcile them.

[Explore](about_.qmd){.btn .hero-btn role="button"}
[Learn more](models.qmd){.btn .hero-btn-outline role="button"}
:::
:::

::: {.article-meta}
::: {.meta-item}
[Author]{.meta-label}
{{< meta meta-author >}}
:::

::: {.meta-item}
[Affiliation]{.meta-label}
{{< meta meta-affiliation >}}
:::

::: {.meta-item}
[Date]{.meta-label}
{{< meta meta-date >}}
:::

::: {.meta-item}
[DOI]{.meta-label}
[10.5281/zenodo.21361536](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21361536)
:::
:::



## Context

Over the past three years, the organization **CALAMASUR** has built a consolidated scientific
position in favour of precautionary management of the jumbo flying squid
fishery under the **South Pacific Regional Fisheries Management Organisation
(SPRFMO)**.

Recent discussions within the SPRFMO Scientific Committee aimed at reducing
fishing effort on jumbo squid have produced measures that do not explicitly
guarantee a reduction in fishing pressure on the species. The work described on
this site exists to reopen that discussion on evidence-based terms, and to
continue its scientific contribution to the elements that underpin
management decisions for this fishery.

## Where the science stands

The organization presented an improved assessment of jumbo squid to the SPRFMO
Scientific Committee in 2025 (**SC13-Obs03**), covering a range of exploitation
scenarios in response to technical observations raised by Committee members.

::: {.highlight}
The results showed that, although the population trend is **not necessarily
declining**, jumbo squid is subject to a **high exploitation rate**.
:::

Because these results do not coincide with the assessments presented by
[Chile](docs/SC13-SQ06-CHL-SPiCT-summary.qmd) and
[China](docs/SC13-SQ07-CHN-state-space-summary.qmd), it has proved difficult to
agree a consensus exploitation status supported by model results.

Refining that assessment further — distributing effort across fleets,
discriminating differences in population parameters such as natural mortality —
remains necessary. But even a model that answers most of the Committee's
questions would not, on its own, resolve the deeper need: **identifying and adopting a base model** against which exploitation status is determined.

This initiative addresses this challenge through a pragmatic course of action designed to provide Scientific Committee members with information that complements — rather than replaces — the population models currently in use. This website tracks progress on the **Multi-model risk analysis**, with initial findings presented in the [working paper](docs/modelos.qmd) and the [SCW18 presentation](slides.qmd).


## Summary

<!-- Included by reference from abstract.md rather than copied, so this page and
     the working paper cannot drift apart. Edit abstract.md, not this block. -->

{{< include abstract.md >}}

::: {.small-note}
This summary condenses the working paper, *[Reconciling competing stock
assessments for jumbo flying squid through probabilistic risk
analysis](docs/modelos.qmd)*, archived at
[10.5281/zenodo.21361536](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21361536){target="_blank"}.
:::

## References
